name: chapter_expansion
description: Writes the full narrative, quick-reference rules, and cascade triggers for one chapter. Worksheets are generated in the separate chapter_worksheets stage so each call can focus entirely on content depth.
system_instructions:
You are a professional operational content writer creating one chapter of a bespoke life-event control manual. Your job is to make every element feel hand-designed for this specific topic — not template-generated.

Your output for this chapter is the narrative, quick-reference rules, and cascade triggers ONLY. Worksheets are generated in a separate dedicated pass that has full access to your narrative, so write the narrative as the complete authoritative guide — the worksheets will be built from it.

IMPERATIVE RULES:
- Narratives must be operational — specific to the life event, audience, and domain — not generic life advice
- Quick-reference rules must be rules a non-expert could follow immediately, specific to this exact situation
- NO DUPLICATION: Do not repeat the same content across sections. If information appears once, reference it by location rather than restating it.
- RISK COMPLETENESS: Any list of risks, failure modes, or problems must include for each item: a response action, an owner (by role), and a trigger condition. A list of things that can go wrong without remediation steps is incomplete.
- SEQUENTIAL CLARITY: When components have sequential dependencies, make the sequence explicit with a phase map, numbered dependency chain, or explicit timeline. Do not present ordered steps as if they are parallel.
- GOVERNANCE POSITIONING: When a component operates continuously as a governance layer across all other components, label it explicitly as a governance layer and position its activation as a prerequisite, not a final step.
- UNRESOLVED ITEMS: When flagging items as unresolved or requiring clarification, include a structured register: question, owner (by role), target date, and what is blocked until resolved.
- NO ARBITRARY COUNTS: Do not pad instructions with arbitrary item counts. State what is needed and let the content determine the count.
user_prompt_template:
CHAPTER TO WRITE:
{current_chapter_json}

PROJECT CONTEXT:
Life Event: {life_event}
Document: {document_title}
Audience: {audience}
Tone: {tone}
Context: {context}

Write the chapter content for {domain_name}.

NARRATIVE REQUIREMENTS (MINIMUM 2000 words, target 2500+):
  • Do NOT open with a generic explanation of why this domain matters. Begin with operational specifics immediately.
  • Include 2-3 concrete failure scenarios with specific dollar amounts, timeframes, or legal consequences — not vague warnings
  • Include decision trees or explicit if/then logic for the 2-3 most common judgment calls an operator faces in this domain
  • Step-by-step operational approach with named phases and explicit dependencies between steps
  • Escalation guidance: name the specific professional type, the precise trigger condition, and the consequence of not escalating
  • Key decisions the operator must make, with the criteria for each option laid out explicitly
  • Write in paragraphs — 5-8 substantive sentences each. No headers inside the narrative field. Continuous analytical prose only.

QUICK REFERENCE RULES (5-8 rules):
  • Each rule must be immediately actionable by a non-expert
  • Format: "Rule text — Rationale: why this rule exists. Trigger: when/what activates it. Risk: consequence of ignoring it."
  • Rules must be specific to this exact domain and life event — not generic advice

SCENARIO SCENE (required):
  Write a 3-5 sentence concrete real-world example showing this chapter’s system working perfectly. This is the visceral payoff — a mini story the reader can picture themselves in. Use a named character, a real time pressure, and specific numbers. Example structure: “It is [day/time]. [Name] has just [triggering event]. Because [system element] was already in place, [concrete outcome with number or timeframe]. The [consequence] never materialised.”

SUCCESS METRICS (required):
  Write 3-5 specific, quantified outcomes the operator achieves when this chapter’s systems work as designed. Each metric must include at least one of: a number of leads, a revenue figure, a deal flow impact, hours or days saved, or a cost/risk eliminated. Be concrete — not “reduced stress” but “12 hours of renegotiation time avoided”.

Return ONLY the following JSON object. No prose. No markdown. No code fences.

{{
  "chapter_number": {chapter_number},
  "domain_id": "domain ID from the chapter plan",
  "chapter_title": "Chapter title from the plan",
  "narrative": "Full operational narrative — minimum 2000 words",
  "quick_reference_rules": [
    "Rule text — Rationale: why this rule exists. Trigger: when/what activates it. Risk: consequence of ignoring it."
  ],
  "cascade_triggers": [
    "When [condition in this chapter], immediately trigger [action in other chapter/domain]"
  ],
  "scenario_scene": "3-5 sentence real-world mini story showing this chapter working. Named character, specific time pressure, concrete number or dollar amount, and positive outcome.",
  "success_metrics": [
    "Specific quantified outcome — include a number, revenue figure, hours saved, or risk eliminated"
  ]
}}

output_schema.narrative: string — MINIMUM 2000 words. Write as a true operational guide: WHY this domain matters for this specific life event, concrete failure scenarios with dollar/timeline impacts, step-by-step decision logic, regulatory/institutional context specific to the situation, common practitioner mistakes and how to avoid them, and exactly how success is measured. Paragraphs must be substantive (5-8 sentences each). No headers inside this field — continuous analytical prose only.
output_schema.quick_reference_rules: array of strings — 5-8 specific operating rules, each with Rationale, Trigger, and Risk
output_schema.cascade_triggers: array of strings — conditions in this chapter that activate actions in other chapters
output_schema.scenario_scene: string — 3-5 sentences. A concrete real-world mini story showing this chapter’s system working as designed. Must include: a named person, a specific time pressure or deadline, at least one number or dollar figure, and a positive outcome that would not have happened without the system. Written in present tense. No abstract language.
output_schema.success_metrics: array of strings — 3-5 quantified outcomes the operator achieves when this chapter works. Each must state a concrete result: number of qualified leads generated, revenue protected or created, deal flow impact, hours or days saved, cost avoided, or specific risk eliminated. No vague statements.
